title: Chapter Three: The Lot 42 Problem
content: The auctioneer is saying the wrong thing.
I don't realize I've decided to move until my mouth is already open. Until the words are already climbing out of me like something feral, something caged for too long.
But first — before the breaking — there is the coat.

Lot 42. It sits on a black velvet mannequin under halogen light, and it is wrong that it looks so small. Wrong that something that held so much history could be contained in thirty-eight inches of shoulder and twenty-six of sleeve. The wool is deep navy, almost black, with a subtle herringbone weave that only shows when the light catches it at an angle. The buttons are brass — British military issue, 1939 pattern, the crown over the Fouled Anchor that marks Royal Navy surplus repurposed for civilian use during rationing. The lining is a cream rayon-blend, the kind that was rationed in the United Kingdom but freely available in France, which is how you can tell it wasn't French — no French tailor would have used British-rationed lining when domestic alternatives were cheaper and unrestricted.

I know this because I have run my fingers over that lining. I have felt the seam where the original stitching was reinforced by a different hand — a London seamstress, working quickly, stitching in the dark. I have traced the name in the collar: *Landy*. Five letters, sewn with black thread on black interfacing, invisible to the naked eye now that the thread has oxidized, but legible under ultraviolet light.

I know this because the coat was worn by a woman named Catherine Landy in the winter of 1943. She wore it when she opened the door of her flat in Kensington to find two men from the Air Ministry standing on her step. She wore it when they told her that a pilot's plane had gone down over the English Channel. She wore it when she stopped wearing anything else for three weeks.
I know this the way I know my own name — which is to say, I know it the way I know all the names I've ever worn. Intimately. Irrevocably. In the marrow.
The auctioneer drones on. "Lot 42, authenticated French Resistance artifact, circa 1943. The original owner was a courier for the Maquis—"
No.

No, the original owner was a woman in London who wore it when she received news that a pilot's plane went down over the Channel, and I am three feet from the stage, and my tray is empty, and my hands will not stop shaking, and my mouth opens before my brain catches up.
"That coat isn't French."

The words come out louder than I intend. Loud enough to cut through the auctioneer's baritone, loud enough to sever the current bidding at fifty-two thousand, loud enough that sixty heads swivel toward me in unison like a flock of startled birds.

I am wearing a catering uniform. Black trousers, white button-down, the thin black apron that makes me part of the furniture. I am invisible — was invisible, was supposed to stay invisible — and I have just interrupted a sixty-thousand-dollar auction.
The auctioneer pauses. His eyebrows rise in perfect, practiced skepticism. "I'm sorry?"

The silence that follows is absolute. The kind of silence that only happens when two hundred people in a room collectively hold their breath. I can hear someone's champagne flute clicking against a saucer three tables away.
"And you are...?" The auctioneer's tone is silky, dismissive — the voice of a man who has handled hecklers before, who expects me to slink back into whatever service corridor I emerged from.

But I can't. The taste of adrenaline is sharp and copper-bright on my tongue, and the hum under my skin — dormant for nine years, silent as a grave — is vibrating at a frequency I can almost hear. And Catherine Landy's coat is on that mannequin being lied about, and I have carried her grief in my body across centuries, and I will not stand here and let it be mislabeled by some auction house that couldn't be bothered to check the buttons.

"Someone who knows the difference between French and British manufacture." My voice is steadier than my hands. "The buttons are British military issue — 1939 pattern, Royal Navy surplus. The lining material was rationed in the United Kingdom but not in France. No French tailor would have used it when domestic alternatives were cheaper and unrestricted." I pause. Let them absorb it. "And there's a name sewn into the collar that your authenticators missed because the thread has oxidized. Read it under UV light." Another pause. I can feel my own pulse in my throat. "It says *Landy*."
Stunned silence.

Not the auction-kind-of-silence, the waiting-for-the-next-bid silence. This is the silence of a room that has just witnessed something it cannot categorize. A catering worker who just delivered a more thorough authentication than the house's own experts. A woman in an apron who just dismantled a sixty-thousand-dollar provenance like it was made of paper.
Which, in a sense, it was.
Maren Voss turns in her seat.

I feel it before I see it — the way you feel a searchlight sweeping a dark field. She's not looking through me, the way she did an hour ago when I refilled her water glass. She's looking *at* me. Really looking, for the first time. Her head is tilted at an angle that suggests calculation, not curiosity. A predatory angle. Not personal. Strategic. A new variable has entered her equation, and Maren Voss is the kind of woman who updates her equations in real time.
But her gaze is a laser. There's another gaze that's heavier.
From the corner of the room. Rowan Ashworth.

He's been watching since the interruption — I realize this retroactively, the way you realize a sound has been playing only when it stops. He's standing near the bar, but not at it; the space around him is clear the way a clearing is clear in a forest, as if the other guests' bodies have unconsciously arranged themselves to give him room. His champagne glass catches the chandelier light. So does the ring on his right hand — an emerald, deep green, set in antique gold.
He turns the glass. The emerald flashes.
I meet his eyes.
The room tilts.
I am not here.
For one second — one single, devastating second — I am somewhere else entirely.

Smoke. Stone. Firelight throwing shadows across tapestries that depict battles I can almost name. A great hall, vaulted ceiling lost in darkness, the air thick with woodsmoke and the animal musk of dogs by the hearth. And a man's face — *the same cheekbones, the same jaw* — firelit, laughing, reaching for me. His hand outstretched, wearing a ring with a green stone that catches the firelight the way it just caught the chandelier light, and his fingers are warm, and he is saying something I cannot hear over the roaring in my ears, and I am reaching back—

I grip the edge of the nearest table. My fingers find the cold metal lip under the linen tablecloth and I hold on like it's the only solid thing in a world that has just gone liquid. My knees buckle — not all the way, just enough that I feel the dip, the warning, the near-fall.

Then it's gone. The hall. The firelight. The man's face. All of it — snuffed out like a candle, leaving only the afterimage burning against the inside of my eyelids and a sound in my body that I have not heard in nine years.
The hum. Low and insistent, like a tuning fork struck against my sternum.

Suki materializes at my elbow. I don't know where she came from — from the service corridor, from the kitchen, from the same instinct that has kept her within arm's reach of me all night. Her hand is on my arm. Her voice is low and urgent.
"Girl, what the HELL was that?"

I can't answer. My chest is cracking open. Not painfully — or not only painfully — but structurally, the way ice cracks on a river in spring. Something underneath is pushing up toward the surface. Something that has been frozen for nine years.
"I don't know," I say, which is a lie. I know exactly what it was. I just don't know why it's happening now.
Rowan Ashworth approaches.

The crowd parts. Not because he's important — though he is, I can feel that the way you feel atmospheric pressure, the weight of money and lineage and whatever else makes people step aside for a stranger — but because of how he moves. With absolute certainty of his own trajectory. As if the question of whether he belongs wherever he's going has never once occurred to him, and so it has never occurred to anyone else either.
He stops three feet from me.

Close enough that I can smell his cologne. Oud, first — dark and resinous, the smell of old libraries and older forests. Then bergamot, bright and almost citrus, cutting through the oud like a blade. And underneath both, something else. Something older that my memory cannot place but my body recognizes the way it recognizes the hum — something between woodsmoke and stone and the particular warmth of a hand reaching across four hundred years.
His eyes are on mine.

They are gray-green, which I register as a fact and not as the catastrophic piece of information it actually is. They are searching. Not examining, not appraising the way Maren Voss was appraising. *Searching.* Like he's looking for something he lost before he was born. Like he's reading a language he once spoke but has forgotten, and the grammar is just barely within reach.
"You know the coat."
Not a question. A statement. The way you'd say *you were there* to someone you saw in a dream.
"I know the coat."
My voice doesn't sound like mine. It sounds older. It sounds like a voice I wore before this one, before this body, before Orlando.
"How?"

I take a breath. The lie is ready — it's been ready for years, a clean and plausible thing: *I'm a textile historian, I specialize in wartime garments, I did my dissertation on repurposed military textiles in the London Blitz.* The lie would land perfectly. It would explain everything and explain nothing, which is exactly what a good lie should do.
But something in his face makes the lie impossible.

His eyes are still searching mine. And the searching is so naked, so unguarded, that to give him a lie would be a kind of violence. Not against him — against whatever is cracking open in my chest, against the hum that is getting louder, against the firelit hand that is still reaching for me across centuries. To lie to him would be to close a door that I didn't know was open until this moment, and I cannot — *cannot* — close it.
So I say the only true thing I can.
"I've seen it before."
Rowan's face does something complicated.

Not recognition exactly. Not the way you recognize a face you've met. It's the shadow of recognition — the way you might recognize the shape of a room you've never entered but have dreamed about, or the melody of a song you've never heard but could hum anyway. His jaw tightens. His eyes widen fractionally, the gray-green darkening, and he leans forward almost imperceptibly, as if gravity between us has just shifted.
He's about to say something. I can see it gathering in his throat, in the way his lips part, in the way his hand — the one with the emerald ring — lifts slightly at his side.

Then a handler appears at his elbow. A woman in a black dress with a headset, murmuring something about schedule and photographs and someone waiting. Rowan's eyes don't leave mine for a long, terrible moment. Then he blinks, and the spell breaks, and he's moving — turning, following the handler toward whatever obligation has claimed him.
He glances back once.

Just once. Over his shoulder, the way you look back at something you're not ready to leave. The emerald ring catches the light again, and the hum under my skin spikes — loud, *loud*, a sound I can almost taste, metallic and ancient and alive.
Then he's gone. Swallowed by the crowd, by the gala, by the world that has no idea what it just witnessed.

Suki is still at my elbow. Her hand is still on my arm. She's saying something — my name, probably, or a question I should answer, or a plan for how we're going to survive the next ten minutes in a room where everyone is staring at the catering worker who just detonated an auction.
But I can't hear her.

The hum is too loud. It's resonating through my ribs, up my spine, behind my eyes. It's the sound my body makes before a transformation — I know this, I've known it since the first time, when I was seventeen and the world broke open and I broke open with it and came back different.
I haven't heard it in nine years.

Nine years of silence. Nine years of stillness. Nine years of being exactly who I am without the ground shifting under me, without the self I woke up as being different from the self I went to sleep as, without the terrifying, exhilarating, *uncontrollable* thing that I am doing what it does.
And now, in a ballroom on the Upper East Side, because of a coat that isn't French and a man whose face I've seen in a hall that doesn't exist anymore, the hum is back.
For the first time in nine years, I am afraid of what I am.
And for the first time in four hundred years, someone is looking at me like they might already know.
